{{short description|Non-clinical mental health condition}}
{{Globalize|date=November 2025}}
'''Caregiver syndrome''' or '''caregiver stress''' is a condition that strongly manifests exhaustion, anger, rage, or guilt resulting from unrelieved [[Caregiver|caring]] for a chronically ill patient.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.clarksvilleonline.com/2007/08/23/caregiver-syndrome-reality-for-many-caregivers-dealing-with-dementia/ |title=Caregiver Syndrome: Reality for many caregivers dealing with Dementia |author=Christine Anne Piesyk |date=2009-08-23 |work=Clarksvilleonline.com |access-date=2009-11-05}}</ref> This condition is not listed in the United States' ''[[Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders]],'' although the term is often used by many healthcare professionals in that country. The equivalent used in many other countries, the [[ICD-11]], does include the condition.<ref name="id.who.int">{{Cite web|url=http://id.who.int/icd/entity/757911124|title=Need for assistance at home and no other household member able to render care|access-date=2020-03-14|archive-date=2014-08-08|archive-url=http://webarchive.loc.gov/all/20140808100506/http://id.who.int/icd/entity/757911124}}</ref>

Over 1 in 5 Americans are providing care to those who are ill, aged, and/or disabled.<ref>{{Cite web|last=AARP|date=May 2020|title=Caregiving in the U.S. 2020|url=https://www.aarp.org/content/dam/aarp/ppi/2020/05/full-report-caregiving-in-the-united-states.doi.10.26419-2Fppi.00103.001.pdf|website=aarp.org}}</ref> Over 13 million caregivers provide care for their own children as well. Caregiver syndrome is acute when caring for an individual with behavioral difficulties such as [[fecal incontinence]], [[memory]] issues, sleep problems, [[wandering (dementia)|wandering]], [[Impulsivity|impulse control problems]], [[executive dysfunction]], and/or [[aggression]]. Typical symptoms of the caregiver syndrome include [[fatigue (medical)|fatigue]], [[insomnia]] and stomach complaints, with the most common symptom being [[Depression (mood)|depression]].

==Signs and symptoms==
Those who are providing care for a friend or family member with a long-term illness undergo what is known as [[chronic stress]]. Caregiving has been shown to affect the [[immune system]]. It was found that caregivers to persons with Dementia particularly Frontal temporal patients were more depressed, and they showed lower life satisfaction than the comparison samples. The caregivers also had higher EBV antibody [[titre]]s and lower percentages of [[T cells]] and T<sub>H</sub> cells.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Esterling |first1=B. A. |last2=Kiecolt-Glaser |first2=J. K. |last3=Glaser |first3=R. |date=May 1996 |title=Psychosocial modulation for cytokine-induced natural killer cell activity in older adults |journal=[[Psychosomatic Medicine (journal)|Psychosomatic Medicine]] |volume=58 |issue=3 |pages=264–272 |pmid=8771626 |url=http://pni.osumc.edu/KG%20Publications%20(pdf)/101.pdf |doi=10.1097/00006842-199605000-00010 |s2cid=21031937 |access-date=2016-08-26 |archive-date=2016-12-24 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161224120344/http://pni.osumc.edu/KG%20Publications%20(pdf)/101.pdf }}</ref> Caregiving has also been shown to have adverse effects on wound repair.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Kiecolt-Glaser |first1=J. K. |last2=Marucha |first2=P. T. |last3=Malarkey |first3=W. B. |last4=Mercado |first4=A. M. |last5=Glaser |first5=R. |date=November 1995 |title=Slowing of wound healing by psychological stress |journal=[[The Lancet]] |volume=346 |issue=8984 |pages=1194–1196 |pmid=7475659 |doi=10.1016/S0140-6736(95)92899-5|s2cid=13005846 |doi-access=free |bibcode=1995Lanc..346.1194K }}</ref> 

Furthermore, these biological vulnerabilities are also evident in younger caregivers, implying that it is not an age and caregiver stress interaction. For example, caregivers of children with developmental disabilities have been found to have lower antibody responses to vaccination compared to age and gender matched non-caregiver controls.<ref name="Gallagher et al. 2009">{{cite journal |last1=Gallagher|first1=Stephen|last2=Phillips |first2=Anna C. |last3=Drayson|first3=M|last4=Carroll|first4=D.|title=Parental caregivers of children with developmental disabilities mount a poor antibody response to pneumococcal vaccination |journal= Brain, Behavior, and Immunity|volume=23 |issue=3 |year=2009 |pages=338–46|doi=10.1016/j.bbi.2008.05.006|pmid=18595654|s2cid=21338363|url=http://pure-oai.bham.ac.uk/ws/files/3677756/Phillips_2009_Brain_Behavior_Immunity.pdf}}</ref> In addition, a higher level of blood pressure has also been observed in those younger caregivers compared to a control group of parents and this particularly strong for those without social support.<ref name="Gallagher & Whiteley. 2012">{{cite journal |last1=Gallagher|first1=Stephen|last2=Whiteley  |first2=Jenny.|title=Social support is associated with blood pressure responses in parents caring for children with developmental disabilities|journal=Research in Developmental Disabilities |volume=33 |issue=6 |year=2012 |pages=2099–105|doi=10.1016/j.ridd.2012.06.007|pmid=22771985|hdl=10344/4373|hdl-access=free}}</ref>

Symptoms include [[depression (mood)|depression]], [[anxiety]], and [[anger]]. Chronic stress can create medical problems including [[high blood pressure]], [[diabetes]], and a compromised immune system.<ref name="CNN" /> The impact may reduce the care-giver's [[life expectancy]].<ref name="SchulzBeach1999">{{cite journal |last1=Schulz |first1=Richard |last2=Beach |first2=Scott R. |title=Caregiving as a Risk Factor for Mortality |journal=[[JAMA (journal)|JAMA]] |volume=282 |issue=23 |year=1999 |pages=2215–9 |issn=0098-7484 |doi=10.1001/jama.282.23.2215|pmid=10605972 |doi-access=free }}</ref>  

According to a UK-based study, almost two out of three carers of people with dementia feel lonely. Most of the carers in the study were family members or friends.<ref>{{Cite journal |date=2020-07-22 |title=Most people caring for relatives with dementia experience loneliness |url=https://evidence.nihr.ac.uk/alert/most-people-caring-for-relatives-with-dementia-experience-loneliness/ |journal=NIHR Evidence |type=Plain English summary |language=en |doi=10.3310/alert_40575|s2cid=243269845 |url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Victor |first1=Christina R. |last2=Rippon |first2=Isla |last3=Quinn |first3=Catherine |last4=Nelis |first4=Sharon M. |last5=Martyr |first5=Anthony |last6=Hart |first6=Nicola |last7=Lamont |first7=Ruth |last8=Clare |first8=Linda |date=2021-07-03 |title=The prevalence and predictors of loneliness in caregivers of people with dementia: findings from the IDEAL programme |journal=Aging & Mental Health |language=en |volume=25 |issue=7 |pages=1232–1238 |doi=10.1080/13607863.2020.1753014 |pmid=32306759 |s2cid=216028843 |issn=1360-7863|doi-access=free |hdl=10454/17813 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>

Caregiver syndrome affects people at any age. For example, elderly caregivers are at a 63 percent higher risk of mortality than non-caregivers who are in the same age group. This trend may be due to elevated levels of stress hormones circulating throughout the body. These levels are similar to someone with [[PTSD]]. Because caregivers have to be so immersed in their roles, with day/night hours, they often have to neglect their own health. They are experiencing high amounts of stress along with grief since the health of their loved one is declining. Since their roles are changing from a partnership or parent/child, etc. relationship to a caregiver and patient relationship, caregivers are turning to online forums such as the [[Alzheimer's Association]] for support. This role change is difficult for many people to make, causing them to experience [[anger]], [[resentment]], and [[guilt (emotion)|guilt]]. It is difficult to provide quality care in this state of stress.<ref name="CNN">{{cite news |title=Exhaustion, anger of caregiving get a name |first=Andree |last=LeRoy |work=Health |publisher=[[CNN]] |date=August 13, 2013 |url=http://www.cnn.com/2007/HEALTH/conditions/08/13/caregiver.syndrome/index.html}}</ref>

The health of caregivers should be monitored in various ways.<ref name="GeorgeGwyther1986">{{cite journal |last1=George |first1=L. K. |last2=Gwyther |first2=L. P. |title=Caregiver Well-Being: A Multidimensional Examination of Family Caregivers of Demented Adults |journal=The Gerontologist |volume=26 |issue=3 |year=1986 |pages=253–259 |issn=0016-9013 |doi=10.1093/geront/26.3.253|pmid=3721232 }}</ref> There are tests for measuring the amount of stress on a caregiver.<ref name="Robinson1983">{{cite journal |last1=Robinson |first1=B. C. |title=Validation of a Caregiver Strain Index |journal=[[The Journals of Gerontology|Journal of Gerontology]] |volume=38 |issue=3 |year=1983 |pages=344–348 |issn=0022-1422 |doi=10.1093/geronj/38.3.344|pmid=6841931 }}</ref>

Caregivers are at risk for adverse effects on their health, due to emotional distress.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Scalan |first1=J.M. |last2=Vitaliano |first2=P.P. |last3=Zhang |first3=J. |last4=Savage |first4=M. |last5=Ochs |first5=H. D. |date=December 2001 |title=Lymphocyte proliferation is associated with gender, caregiving, and psychosocial variables in older adults |journal=[[Journal of Behavioral Medicine]] |volume=24 |issue=6 |pages=537–559 |pmid=11778349 |doi=10.1023/A:1012987226388|s2cid=10664679 }}</ref> Even after caregiving has terminated, these stressors can have long-lasting effects on the caregiver's body due to these immune alterations.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Esterling |first1=B. A. |last2=Kiecolt-Glaser |first2=J. K. |last3=Bodnar |first3=J. C. |last4=Glaser |first4=R. |date=July 1994 |title=Chronic stress, social support, and persistent alterations in the natural killer cell response to cytokines in older adults |journal=[[Health Psychology (journal)|Health Psychology]] |volume=13 |issue=4 |pages=291–298 |pmid=7957007 |doi=10.1037/0278-6133.13.4.291 |url=https://interferon.osumc.edu/KG%20Publications%20(pdf)/083.pdf |access-date=2016-08-26 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161011044357/https://interferon.osumc.edu/KG%20Publications%20(pdf)/083.pdf |archive-date=2016-10-11 }}</ref>

Since caregiving can further erode the caregiver's own health,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Gallagher |first1=S. |last2=Phillips |first2=A. C. |last3=Drayson |first3=M. T. |last4=Carroll |first4=D. |date=2009 |title=Caregiving for children with developmental disabilities is associated with a poor antibody response to influenza vaccination |journal=Psychosomatic Medicine |volume=71 |issue=3 |pages=341–344 |pmid=19297308 |doi=10.1097/PSY.0b013e31819d1910|s2cid=38967408 |url=http://pure-oai.bham.ac.uk/ws/files/3677818/Phillips_2009_Pychosomatic_Medicine.pdf }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=von Kaenel |first1=R. |last2=Dimsdale |first2=J. E. |last3=Patternson |first3=T. L. |last4=Grant |first4=I. |date=August 2003 |title=Acute pro-coagulant stress response as a dynamic measure of allostatic load in Alzheimer's Caregivers |journal=[[Annals of Behavioral Medicine]] |volume=26 |issue=1 |pages=42–48 |pmid=12867353 |doi=10.1207/S15324796ABM2601_06|s2cid=3901985 |doi-access=free }}</ref> many studies are being done to assess the risks that a caregiver poses when they assume this job and its effects on their immune functioning,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Li |first1=J. |last2=Cowden |first2=L. G. |last3=King |first3=J. D. |last4=Briles |first4=D. A. |last5=Schroeder |first5=H. W. |last6=Stevens |first6=A. B. |last7=Perry |first7=R. T. |last8=Chen |first8=Z. |last9=Simmons |first9=M.S. |last10=Wiener |first10=H.W. |last11=Tiwari |first11=H.K. |last12=Harrell |first12=L.E. |last13=Go |first13=R.C. |display-authors=3 |date=July–August 2007 |title=Effects of chronic stress and interleukin-10 gene polymorphism on antibody response to tetanus vaccine in family caregivers of patients with Alzheimer's Disease |journal=Psychosomatic Medicine |volume=69 |issue=6 |pages=551–559 |pmid=17634568 |doi=10.1097/PSY.0b013e3180cc2c61|s2cid=23351702 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Redwine |first1=L. |last2=Mills |first2=P. J. |last3=Sada |first3=M. |last4=Dimsdale |first4=J. |last5=Patterson |first5=T. |last6=Grant |first6=I. |display-authors=3 |date=September–October 2004 |title=Differential immune cell chemotaxis responses to acute psychological stress in Alzheimer's caregivers compared to non-caregiver controls |journal=Psychosomatic Medicine |volume=66 |issue=5 |pages=770–775 |pmid=15385705 |doi=10.1097/01.psy.0000138118.62018.87|citeseerx=10.1.1.539.201 |s2cid=31527781 }}</ref> endocrine functioning,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Mausbach |first1=D. T. |last2=Dimsdale |first2=J. E. |last3=Ziegler |first3=M. G. |last4=Mills |first4=P. J. |last5=Ancoli-Israel |first5=S. |last6=Patterson |first6=T. L. |last7=Grant |first7=I. |display-authors=3 |date=July–August 2005 |title=Depressive symptoms predict norepinephrine response to a psychological stressor task in Alzheimer's caregivers |journal=Psychosomatic Medicine |volume=67 |issue=4 |pages=638–642 |pmid=16046380 |doi=10.1097/01.psy.0000173312.90148.97|citeseerx=10.1.1.546.1180 |s2cid=10640643 }}</ref> risk for depression,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Mintzer |first1=J. E. |last2=Robert |first2=M. P. |last3=Loewenstein |first3=D. |last4=Gamez |first4=E. |last5=Millor |first5=A. |last6=Quinteros |first6=R. |last7=Flores |first7=L. |last8=Miller |first8=M |last9=Rainerman |first9=A. |last10=Eisdorfer |first10=C. |display-authors=3 |date=August 1992 |title=Daughters caregiving for Hispanic and non-Hispanic Alzheimer patients: Does ethnicity make a difference? |journal=Community Mental Health Journal |volume=28 |issue=4 |pages=293–303 |pmid=1643838 |doi=10.1007/BF00755796|s2cid=28810226 }}</ref> poor quality of sleep,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Brummett |first1=B. H. |last2=Babyak |first2=M. A. |last3=Siegler |first3=I. C. |last4=Vitaliano |first4=P. P. |last5=Ballard |first5=E. L. |last6=Gwyther |first6=L. P. |last7=Williams |first7=R.B. |display-authors=3 |date=March 2006 |title=Associations among perceptions of social support, negative affect, and quality of sleep in caregivers and noncaregivers |journal=Health Psychology |volume=25 |issue=2 |pages=220–225 |pmid=16569114 |doi=10.1037/0278-6133.25.2.220}}</ref> long-term changes in stress responses,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Grant |first1=L. |last2=Adler |first2=K. A. |last3=Patterson |first3=T. L. |last4=Dimsdale |first4=J. E. |last5=Zeigler |first5=M. G. |last6=Irwin |first6=M. R. |display-authors=3 |date=May–June 2002 |title=Health consequences of Alzheimer's caregiving transitions: Effects of placement and bereavement |journal=Psychosomatic Medicine |volume=64 |issue=3 |pages=477–486 |pmid=12021421 |doi=10.1097/00006842-200205000-00012|citeseerx=10.1.1.495.5259 |s2cid=41444518 }}</ref> Cardiovascular diseases,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Mausbach |first1=B. T. |last2=Patterson |first2=T. L. |last3=Rabinowitz |first3=Y. G. |last4=Grant |first4=L. |last5=Schulz |first5=R.|display-authors=3 |date=September 2007 |title=Depression and distress predict time to cardiovascular disease in dementia caregivers |journal=Health Psychology |volume=26 |issue=5 |pages=539–544 |pmid=17845105 |doi=10.1037/0278-6133.26.5.539|url=https://zenodo.org/record/1231482 }}</ref> an increased risk of infectious disease, and even death.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Brummett |first1=B. H. |last2=Siegler |first2=I. C. |last3=Rohe |first3=W. M. |last4=Barefoot |first4=J. C. |last5=Vitaliano |first5=P. P. |last6=Surwit |first6=R. S. |last7=Feinglos |first7=M.N. |last8=Williams |first8=R.B. |display-authors=3 |date=September–October 2005 |title=Neighborhood characteristics moderate effects of caregiving on glucose functioning |journal=Psychosomatic Medicine |volume=67 |issue=5 |pages=752–758 |pmid=16204434 |doi=10.1097/01.psy.0000174171.24930.11|s2cid=3680007 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Kim |first1=Jung-Hyun |last2=Knight |first2=Bob G. |last3=Longmire |first3=Crystal V. Flynn |date=September 2007 |title=The role of familism in stress and coping processes among African America and White dementia caregivers: Effects on mental and physical health |journal=Health Psychology |volume=26 |issue=5 |pages=564–576 |pmid=17845108 |doi=10.1037/0278-6133.26.5.564}}</ref> Resentment from the patient is what may lead to the depression and distress typically seen in caregivers.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Newsom |first1=J. T. |last2=Schulz |first2=R. |date=March 1998 |title=Caregiving from the recipient's perspective: Negative Reactions to being helped |journal=Health Psychology |volume=17 |issue=2 |pages=172–181 |pmid=9548708 |doi=10.1037/0278-6133.17.2.172}}</ref> This anxiety and depression can then lead back into the health of the caregiver.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Shewick |first1=R. M. |last2=Richards |first2=J. S. |last3=Elliot |first3=T. R. |date=March 1998 |title=Dynamic processes in health outcomes among caregivers of patients with spinal cord injuries |journal=Health Psychology |volume=17 |issue=2 |pages=125–129 |pmid=9548703 |doi=10.1037/0278-6133.17.2.125}}</ref> Generally, research suggests that this role can result in an overall decrease in the quality of life for individuals whom take it on.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Hatzmann |first1=Janneke |last2=Heymans |first2=Hugo S. A. |last3=Ferrer-i-Carbonell |first3=Ada |last4=van Praag |first4=Bernard M. S. |last5=Grootenhuis |first5=Martha A. |date=2008-11-01 |title=Hidden Consequences of Success in Pediatrics: Parental Health-Related Quality of Life—Results From the Care Project |url=https://publications.aap.org/pediatrics/article/122/5/e1030/71556/Hidden-Consequences-of-Success-in-Pediatrics |journal=Pediatrics |language=en |volume=122 |issue=5 |pages=e1030–e1038 |doi=10.1542/peds.2008-0582 |pmid=18852185 |issn=0031-4005|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Senses Dinc |first1=Gulser |last2=Cop |first2=Esra |last3=Tos |first3=Tulay |last4=Sari |first4=Eyup |last5=Senel |first5=Saliha |date=2019-07-03 |title=Mothers of 0–3-year-old children with Down syndrome: Effects on quality of life |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/ped.13936 |journal=Pediatrics International |language=en |volume=61 |issue=9 |pages=865–871 |doi=10.1111/ped.13936 |pmid=31267616 |issn=1328-8067|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Patty |first1=Nathalie J. S. |last2=van Meeteren |first2=Karen M. |last3=Verdonk |first3=Minke |last4=Ketelaar |first4=Marjolijn |last5=Schuengel |first5=Carlo |last6=Willemen |first6=Agnes M. |date=2024-12-15 |title=Conceptualizing burnout from the perspective of parents of children with complex care needs |journal=PEC Innovation |volume=5 |article-number=100325 |doi=10.1016/j.pecinn.2024.100325 |pmid=39161625 |pmc=11332200 |issn=2772-6282}}</ref> 

The [[World Health Organization]]'s categorisation of health conditions, the [[ICD-11]], has a category of "QF27 Difficulty or need for assistance at home and no other household member able to render care".<ref name="id.who.int"/> Its browser and coding tool also associate this condition with the term "caregiver burnout",<ref>https://icd.who.int/ct11/icd11_mms/en/release {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230601050935/https://icd.who.int/ct11/icd11_mms/en/release |date=2023-06-01 }} and search for "burnout"</ref> connecting it to [[occupational burnout]].

=== Caregiver burnout ===

Bodies such as the United States government's [[Centers for Disease Control and Prevention]],<ref>{{cite web |title=Dealing With Diabetes Burnout |url=https://www.cdc.gov/diabetes/library/spotlights/diabetes-burnout.html |website=Centers for Disease Control and Prevention |language=en-us |date=12 August 2019}}</ref> the [[American Diabetes Association]],<ref>{{cite book |title=Diabetes burnout: what to do when you can't take it anymore |publisher=American Diabetes Association |isbn=1-58040-033-7 |date=1999}}</ref> and ''Diabetes Singapore''<ref name="What is Diabetes Burnout">{{cite web |title=What is Diabetes Burnout? |url=https://www.moneyfm893.sg/whats-on/what-is-diabetes-burnout/ |website=MONEY FM 89.3 |date=5 December 2019}}</ref> identify and promote the phenomenon of "diabetes burnout". This relates to the self-care of people with diabetes, particularly those with type-2 diabetes. "Diabetes burnout speaks to the physical and emotional exhaustion that people with diabetes experience when they have to deal with caring for themselves on a day-to-day basis. When you have to do so many things to stay in control then it does take a toll on your emotions... Once they get frustrated, some of them give up and stop (maintaining) a healthy diet, taking their medications regularly, going for exercises and this will result in poor diabetes control."<ref name="What is Diabetes Burnout"/>

==Causes==
Caregiver syndrome is caused by the overwhelming duty of caring for a disabled or chronically ill person. Caregiver stress is caused by an increased stress hormone level for an extended period of time. Caregivers also suffer the grief of a declining loved one, as causing a depressive exhaustive state, deteriorating emotional and mental health. "Double-duty caregivers" are those already working in the healthcare field who feel obligated to also care for their loved ones at home. This over-exhaustion and constant caregiving role can cause an increase in physical and mental health deterioration. It is actually being thought that a part of the stress of being a caregiver is from how they feel about the job. In other words, if a caregiver does not like or want to be a caregiver, they will inflict more stress on themselves by accepting the role.<ref>{{cite web |last=Tunajek |first=Sandra |title=Understanding Caregiver Stress Syndrome |work=Wellness Milestones |publisher=[[American Association of Nurse Anesthetists]] |date=October 2010 |url=http://www.aana.com/resources2/health-wellness/Documents/nb_milestone_1010.pdf |access-date=2016-08-26 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160919190040/http://www.aana.com/resources2/health-wellness/Documents/nb_milestone_1010.pdf |archive-date=2016-09-19 }}</ref> Support from the religious community is directly and negatively associated with anger.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Márquez-González|first1=María|last2=López|first2=Javier|last3=Romero-Moreno|first3=Rosa|last4=Losada|first4=Andrés|date=2012|title=Anger, Spiritual Meaning and Support from the Religious Community in Dementia Caregiving|journal=Journal of Religion and Health|language=en|volume=51|issue=1|pages=179–186|doi=10.1007/s10943-010-9362-7|pmid=20467814|s2cid=1906691|issn=0022-4197}}</ref>

===Risks===
The American Academy of Geriatric Psychiatrists reports one out of four American families provide care for a family member over the age of 50. By 2030, the U.S. Census Bureau estimates a population of 71 million Americans over 65.<ref name="CNN"/> In the U.K., over 450,000 dementia patients are cared for at home. Nevertheless, over half of the caregivers (52.6%) indicated that they had some desire to institutionalize their relatives with dementia.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=López|first1=J.|last2=Losada|first2=A.|last3=Romero-Moreno|first3=R.|last4=Márquez-González|first4=M.|last5=Martínez-Martín|first5=P.|date=2010|title=Factors associated with dementia caregivers' preference for institutional care|journal=Neurología (English Edition)|language=en|volume=27|issue=2|pages=83–89|doi=10.1016/j.nrleng.2012.03.004|doi-access=}}</ref>

The [[American Academy of Family Physicians]] and the National Center on Caregiving both believe all caregivers should be screened for stress and depression and recommend providing caregivers with their own resources to help them cope.<ref name="CNN" />

Since family (and more often one member) most often assumes the primary caregiver role, these strains fall upon them. Care for those who are chronically ill is irregular, so there are not many facilities that can provide adequate care. This caregiving role is more commonly assumed by women than men. Since there are some illnesses that create a more intense need for caregiving, the caregiver is responsible for almost every aspect in the patient's life. One of the positive aspects of caregiving for a loved one is that it can improve their quality of life, but when the caregiver is depleted of confidence, the recovery may be hindered.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Molloy |first1=G. J. |last2=Jonhston |first2=M. |last3=Johnston |first3=D. W. |last4=Pollard |first4=B. |last5=Morrison |first5=V. |last6=Bonetti |first6=D. |last7=Joice |first7=S. |last8=MacWalter |first8=R. |display-authors=3 |date=March 2008 |title=Spousal caregiver confidence and recovery from ambulatory activity limitations in stroke survivors |journal=Health Psychology |volume=27 |issue=2 |pages=286–290 |pmid=18377149 |doi=10.1037/0278-6133.27.2.286}}</ref>

Parents of children with [[Congenital heart defect|CHD]] experience [[Mental distress|psychological distress]]&nbsp;such as high levels of&nbsp;caregiver stress, [[anxiety]]&nbsp;and&nbsp;[[Depression (mood)|depression]] compared to parents of children without complex needs.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Patty |first1=Nathalie J. S. |last2=van Meeteren |first2=Karen M. |last3=Verdonk |first3=Minke |last4=Ketelaar |first4=Marjolijn |last5=Schuengel |first5=Carlo |last6=Willemen |first6=Agnes M. |date=2024-12-15 |title=Conceptualizing burnout from the perspective of parents of children with complex care needs |journal=PEC Innovation |volume=5 |article-number=100325 |doi=10.1016/j.pecinn.2024.100325 |pmid=39161625 |pmc=11332200 |issn=2772-6282}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Soulvie|first1=M. Amber|last2=Desai|first2=Priti P.|last3=White|first3=Carmel Parker|last4=Sullivan|first4=Brittany N.|date=2012-07-01|title=Psychological Distress Experienced by Parents of Young Children With Congenital Heart Defects: A Comprehensive Review of Literature|journal=Journal of Social Service Research|volume=38|issue=4|pages=484–502|doi=10.1080/01488376.2012.696410|s2cid=143724775|issn=0148-8376}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Kütük |first1=Meryem Özlem |last2=Tufan |first2=Ali Evren |last3=Kılıçaslan |first3=Fethiye |last4=Güler |first4=Gülen |last5=Çelik |first5=Fatma |last6=Altıntaş |first6=Ebru |last7=Gökçen |first7=Cem |last8=Karadağ |first8=Mehmet |last9=Yektaş |first9=Çiğdem |last10=Mutluer |first10=Tuba |last11=Kandemir |first11=Hasan |last12=Büber |first12=Ahmet |last13=Topal |first13=Zehra |last14=Acikbas |first14=Ufuk |last15=Giray |first15=Aslı |date=2021-11-01 |title=High Depression Symptoms and Burnout Levels Among Parents of Children with Autism Spectrum Disorders: A Multi-Center, Cross-Sectional, Case–Control Study |journal=Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders |language=en |volume=51 |issue=11 |pages=4086–4099 |doi=10.1007/s10803-021-04874-4 |pmid=33459915 |issn=1573-3432|hdl=20.500.12868/710 |hdl-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Scherer |first1=Nathaniel |last2=Verhey |first2=Ibone |last3=Kuper |first3=Hannah |date=2019-07-30 |title=Depression and anxiety in parents of children with intellectual and developmental disabilities: A systematic review and meta-analysis |journal=PLOS ONE |language=en |volume=14 |issue=7 |article-number=e0219888 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0219888 |doi-access=free |issn=1932-6203 |pmc=6667144 |pmid=31361768|bibcode=2019PLoSO..1419888S }}</ref>

Caregiving for military service members who have experienced a [[traumatic brain injury]] or [[PTSD]] can be very challenging as well. On April 21, 2010, the U.S. Congress passed what is known as the "[[Caregivers and Veterans Omnibus Health Services Act of 2010]]". This act recognizes the importance of caregivers who are caring for Veterans, and established a program of assistance for them with benefits including covering counseling and mental health services under the benefits of [[United States Department of Veterans Affairs|Department of Veterans Affairs]].<ref name="Care for Caregivers">{{cite web |url=https://www.hnfs.net/content/hnfs/home/tn/prov/provider_news/care_caregivers.html |archive-url=https://archive.today/20130413201905/https://www.hnfs.net/content/hnfs/home/tn/prov/provider_news/care_caregivers.html |archive-date=2013-04-13 |title=Care for Caregivers |publisher=[[Health Net]] |access-date=2013-02-27}}</ref>

==Issues in health care==
Since this term, "Caregiver syndrome" is widely used among physicians, but it is not mentioned in the ''Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders'' (DSM) or in medical literature, physicians are not always sure how to approach the issues that arise with this syndrome. Therefore, this is not addressed frequently. In a survey given by the American Academy of Family Physicians, they found that fewer than 50 percent of caregivers were asked by their doctors whether or not they were experiencing caregiver stress. If this were listed in the DSM with an official diagnosis, it could possibly stigmatize those who have it. Many believe it would be beneficial for this to receive a clinical name, though, so caregivers would be able to receive the appropriate resources they need. This would encourage health care professionals to develop better strategies for treatment of Caregiver Syndrome, as well as requiring health insurance agencies to pay for appropriate treatment.

Although previous studies indicate a negative association between caregivers' anger and health, the potential mechanisms linking this relationship are not yet fully understood.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=López|first1=J.|last2=Romero-Moreno|first2=R.|last3=Márquez-González|first3=M.|last4=Losada|first4=A.|date=2015|title=Anger and Health in Dementia Caregivers: Exploring the Mediation Effect of Optimism|journal=Stress and Health|language=en|volume=31|issue=2|pages=158–165|doi=10.1002/smi.2539|pmid=24123699|issn=1532-2998|doi-access=free}}</ref>

==Prevention==
Effective [[coping strategies]] such as sleep, exercise and relaxation can help prevent stress.<ref>{{cite web |title=Caring for Yourself |publisher=Assurance Home Care |date=May 11, 2016 |url=http://assurancehomecare.ca/blog/2016/5/11/caring-for-yourself}}</ref> Caregivers fare better when they have active coping skills,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Aschbacher |first1=K. |last2=Patterson |first2=T. L. |last3=von Kanel |first3=R. |last4=Dimsdale |first4=J. E. |last5=Mills |first5=P. J. |last6=Adler |first6=K. A. |last7=Ancoli-Israel |first7=S. |last8=Grant |first8=I. |display-authors=3 |date=November–December 2005 |title=Coping processes and hemostatic reactivity to acute stress in dementia caregivers |journal=Psychosomatic Medicine |volume=67 |issue=6 |pages=964–971 |pmid=16314602 |doi=10.1097/01.psy.0000188458.85597.bc|s2cid=8060004 }}</ref> such as these coping interventions:
[[mindfulness-based stress reduction]], [[writing therapy]], coping effectiveness training, [[stress management]], relaxation training, and [[Assistive Technology]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Assistive Technology |date=July 2, 2019 |url=https://www.alzyouneed.com}}</ref> For example, a 2025 study found that informal caregivers who engaged in repeated self-disclosure to a social robot experienced reduced stress and loneliness, improved mood, and greater acceptance of their caregiving roles, suggesting potential benefits of social robots as emotional support tools for caregivers.<ref>Laban, G., Morrison, V., Kappas, A., & Cross, E. S. (2025). Coping with emotional distress via self-disclosure to robots: An intervention with caregivers. International Journal of Social Robotics. https://doi.org/10.1007/s12369-024-01207-0</ref>

Nearly 15 million Americans provide care that is unpaid to a person living with Dementia. Alzheimer's disease is the most commonly diagnosed type but research says that caring for a person with [[Frontotemporal Dementia]] is more burdensome on carers.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Mioshi |first1=Eneida |last2=Bristow |first2=Matt |last3=Cook |first3=Rachel |last4=Hodges |first4=John R. |date=2009 |title=Factors Underlying Caregiver Stress in Frontotemporal Dementia and Alzheimer's Disease |url=https://www.karger.com/Article/FullText/193626 |journal=Dementia and Geriatric Cognitive Disorders |language=en |volume=27 |issue=1 |pages=76–81 |doi=10.1159/000193626 |pmid=19155621 |s2cid=38425232 |issn=1420-8008|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Early onset Dementia has even greater difficulties for carers. In many cases carers are overburdened and not supported and their health suffers. In order to maintain their own well-being, caregivers need to focus on their own needs. They need to take time for their own health, and get the appropriate support that they need such as respite from their care-giving duties. Through training, caregivers can learn how to handle the behaviors that are challenging them, and improve their own communication skills. The most important thing the caregiver can do is keep the person with Alzheimer's safe. Research has shown that caregivers experience lower stress and better health when they learn skills through this caregiving training and participate in support groups. Participating in these groups allows caregivers to care for their family members longer in their homes.<ref name="FCA">{{cite web |title=Alzheimer's Disease & Caregiving |publisher=[[Family Caregiver Alliance]] |access-date=February 27, 2013 |url=http://www.caregiver.org/caregiver/jsp/content_node.jsp?nodeid=567}}</ref>

A 2014 Cochrane review found that telephone counseling can reduce symptoms of depression for caregivers and address other important caregiver needs.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|title=Efficacy and Experiences of Telephone Counselling for Informal Carers of People With Dementia|last1=S|first1=Lins|last2=D|first2=Hayder-Beichel|date=2014-09-01|journal=The Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews|language=en|pmid=25177838|last3=G|first3=Rücker|last4=E|first4=Motschall|last5=G|first5=Antes|last6=G|first6=Meyer|last7=G|first7=Langer|volume=2014|issue=9|article-number=CD009126|doi=10.1002/14651858.CD009126.pub2|pmc=7433299}}</ref>

=== Remotely delivered information for caregivers ===
A 2021 Cochrane review found that remotely delivered interventions including support, training and information may reduce the burden for the informal caregiver and improve their depressive symptoms.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=González-Fraile|first1=Eduardo|last2=Ballesteros|first2=Javier|last3=Rueda|first3=José-Ramón|last4=Santos-Zorrozúa|first4=Borja|last5=Solà|first5=Ivan|last6=McCleery|first6=Jenny|date=2021-01-04|title=Remotely delivered information, training and support for informal caregivers of people with dementia|journal=Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews|volume=1|issue=1 |article-number=CD006440|doi=10.1002/14651858.cd006440.pub3|issn=1465-1858|pmc=8094510|pmid=33417236}}</ref> However, there is no certain evidence that they improve health-related quality of life. The findings are based on moderate certainty evidence from 26 studies.

===REACH Program===
The Resources for Enhancing Alzheimer's Caregiver Health (REACH) Project was created in 1995. This project was designed to enhance family caregiving for those who were taking care of relatives that have Alzheimer's disease and other related dementia (ADRD). This program includes: support groups, behavioral skills training programs, and family-based systems interventions.{{cn|date=February 2025}}

This program was designed specifically for people who are caring for a loved one with Alzheimer's Disease or Dementia at home, and makes it possible for those with dementia to live in the own homes longer by addressing these problems of caregiver health that force the caregiver to move their loved ones to assisted-living facilities. If they can manage the challenges that come along with caregiving better, both will benefit from this. Special one-on-one training is provided for the caregiver, as well as counseling. This allows them to be more effective in their caregiving roles. They receive help directly from dementia care specialists who work with the client on an individual basis to find solutions to problems such as:
caregiver stress, challenging behaviors, home safety, depression, self care, and social support.<ref name="Alz">{{cite web |url=http://www.alz.org/northcentraltexas/in_my_community_reach.asp |title=Resources for Enhancing Alzheimer's Caregiver Health (REACH) |publisher=Alzheimer's Association |access-date=2013-04-03 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517050005/http://www.alz.org/northcentraltexas/in_my_community_reach.asp |archive-date=2013-05-17 }}</ref>

==Benefits of caregiving==
Caregiving can actually provide a health advantage as well for some caregivers. Caregivers maintained higher physical performance when compared to non-caregivers. They declined less in tasks than the low-intensity caregivers and non-caregivers such as: walking pace, grip strength, and the speed with which they could rise from a chair. Caregivers also did significantly better on memory tasks than did non-caregivers over a 2-year time frame. Caregivers scored at the level of someone 10 years younger than them, although both groups (caregivers vs. non-caregivers) were both in their eighties.<ref name="Benefits">{{cite web |url=http://newoldage.blogs.nytimes.com/2011/10/12/caregivings-hidden-benefits/ |title=Caregiving's Hidden Benefits |first=Paula |last=Span |work=The New York Times |date=October 12, 2011}}</ref>

While this role brings with it high costs, high rewards are also there too. This is known as "Caregiver gain". These rewards are emotional, psychological, and spiritual such as: growing confidence in one's ability, feelings of personal satisfaction and increased family closeness.<ref name="Benefits"/>

Women who become caregivers are healthy enough to take on the task, therefore it makes sense that they would be stronger than their non-caregiver counterparts, and remain stronger than them. The demands of caregiving cause caregivers to move around a lot, and stay on their feet. Therefore, exercise can improve both physical health and [[cognition]]. The complex thought as required by caregiving can ward off cognitive decline. This includes activities such as: monitoring medications, scheduling, and financial responsibilities.<ref name="Benefits"/>

Other benefits mentioned by caregivers are that it gives their life meaning, and produces pride in their success as a caregiver. They are also able to give back to someone else. It has also been noted that psychological [[benefit finding]] can be an important way of dealing with stress.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Folkman |first1=S. |last2=Moskowitz |first2=J. T. |date=June 2000 |title=Positive affect and the other side of coping |journal=[[American Psychologist]] |volume=55 |issue=6 |pages=647–654 |pmid=10892207 |doi=10.1037/0003-066X.55.6.647|bibcode=2000AmPsy..55..647F |citeseerx=10.1.1.596.8982 }}</ref> The Perceived Benefits of Caregiving scale includes 11 items with questions such as, "Has caregiving given more meaning to your life?" and "Has caregiving made you feel important?" There was an alpha coefficient of 0.7 for this scale.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Beach |first1=S. R. |last2=Schulz |first2=J. L. |last3=Yee |first3=J. L. |last4=Jackson |first4=S. |date=June 2000 |title=Negative and positive health effects of caring for a disabled spouse: longitudinal findings from the Caregiver Health Effects Study |journal=[[Psychology and Aging]] |volume=15 |issue=2 |pages=42–53 |pmid=10879581 |doi=10.1037/0882-7974.15.2.259|citeseerx=10.1.1.536.5145 }}</ref> These benefits of caregiving have been found to be associated with improved caregiver adaptation to those who are caring for someone with dementia, end of life caregiving, and bereavement.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Haley |first1=W.E. |last2=LaMonde |first2=L.A. |last3=Han |first3=B. |last4=Burton |first4=A.M. |last5=Schonwetter |first5=R. |date=April 2003 |title=Predictors of depression and life satisfaction among spousal caregivers in hospice: Application of a stress process model |journal=[[Journal of Palliative Medicine]] |volume=6 |issue=2 |pages=215–224 |pmid=12854938 |doi=10.1089/109662103764978461}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Boerner |first1=K. |last2=Schulz |first2=R. |last3=Horowitz |first3=A. |date=December 2004 |title=Positive aspects of caregiving and adaptation to bereavement |journal=Psychology and Aging |volume=19 |issue=4 |pages=668–675 |pmid=15584791 |doi=10.1037/0882-7974.19.4.668}}</ref> A study done with dementia caregivers showed that finding the benefits in caregiving predicted a better response to a caregiver intervention over a time period of 12 months.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hilgeman |first1=M. M. |last2=Allen |first2=R. S. |last3=DeCoster |first3=J. |last4=Burgio |first4=L.D. |date=June 2007 |title=Positive aspects of caregiving as a moderator of treatment outcome over 12 months |journal=Psychology and Aging |volume=22 |issue=2 |pages=361–371 |pmid=17563191 |pmc=2579267 |doi=10.1037/0882-7974.22.2.361}}</ref>
